title: Same Tree
statement: Given the roots of two binary trees, determine whether the trees have exactly the same shape and the same value at every corresponding node.
constraints: Each tree has 0 to 100 nodes. Node values are integers from -10^4 to 10^4. An O(n) traversal is sufficient.

brute_force.approach: Serialize both trees into lists that include node values and explicit markers for missing children, then compare the two serialized lists. The missing-child markers are necessary because values alone do not capture structure.
brute_force.time: O(n + m), where n and m are the numbers of nodes in the two trees.
brute_force.space: O(n + m) for the serialized forms.

optimal.approach: Compare the two current nodes directly. If both are missing, that position matches. If exactly one is missing, the trees differ. If both exist, their values must match, and the same check must succeed for their left children and their right children.
optimal.time: O(k), where k is the number of corresponding positions checked; in the worst case of equal or nearly equal trees, O(n).
optimal.space: O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is tree height; O(n) in the worst case for a skewed tree.

key_insight: Tree equality can be decided locally at each matching position: the two nodes must both be missing, or both exist with the same value and matching left and right child pairs.
ladder: compare one position at a time
apply the same check to child pairs

traps.wrong_approach: Compare only the sequence of node values from a traversal.
traps.why_wrong: The same values in the same traversal order can come from different shapes if missing children are not represented.
traps.counterexample: p = [1,2], q = [1,null,2] both can produce values [1,2], but the trees are different.

traps.wrong_approach: Check only that the shapes match.
traps.why_wrong: Two trees can have identical structure but different values at corresponding nodes.
traps.counterexample: p = [1,2,1], q = [1,1,2] have the same shape but different child values.

traps.wrong_approach: Return true whenever either current node is missing.
traps.why_wrong: If one side has a node and the other side does not, the structure differs.

traps.wrong_approach: Compare sorted values or value counts.
traps.why_wrong: The placement of values matters, not just which values appear.
traps.counterexample: p = [1,2,3], q = [1,3,2] have the same values but are not the same tree.

leak_terms: same tree iff roots match and both subtrees match
underlying_primitive: Breaking a tree question into the current node comparison plus the same question on the matching left and right child pairs.
